speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: I don't think there's any doubt there's people who say that he saved the bank and I don't know what they would have done. I know when I heard people, these people that we had made the list for to go out and see, they looked at him. They told me that afterwards. It's a good thing they brought Frank Green. Axel Bowman was not a popular man. Ollie Bowman was popular. He was kind. He was not a banker, but he was respected, yes, but he was destitute. You can imagine a man going out with his feeling when he went to get this money because it looked bad for him. It was his reflection. Will you come to my rescue?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: That's just exactly what he was trying to say. But he didn't. He didn't do the talking. He probably did the talking. Axel was a talker. He probably did the talking. Ollie stayed in the background because he felt that my days were over. He never was the same man after that either. He was disheartened. He had lost all he had built. He snapped out of it pretty good because it was so successful. It started with a bang again, and everything fell in its place. Sure, there was a loss, but you just forget it. He hated Wheeler. He hated the bang examiner. Of course, he had to blame somebody, which was natural. I didn't. I looked at it from the other side. If it wouldn't have been Wheeler, it would have been somebody else, and it could have been worse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that saying? I remember reading something like the bang examiner.

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Wheeler said, Well, we cleaned up a lot of paper, and Ollie Bowman said, Yeah, at my expense or something. Yes, that's how he always felt. We did get it back. The new people who put up the money, it was business. He had no choice. He had seventy shares. That meant he was to go out and find two hundred and fifty dollars a share. That was $17 ,500. That was a fortune in those days. That was unbelievable. He cashed in his life insurance. That was the means that he had to do it. He cashed in his life insurance. I don't think he sold any land, but in order for him to save what he could, he saved every share of stock that he could raise and pay the $250 assessment on. He tried. He did everything in his power. When he got through, the rest of them divided up among W .A. Keeney, among John Nylund, among Mons Johnson, and among Elif Johnson, that's four. Carl Johnson is five. Albert Lewis is six. Frank Green is seven, and myself was eight. There was eight, and Ollie Bowman was nine.

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: I can't remember that there was another person. In other words, Ollie was first. He got whatever he could stand, and the rest was divided up. You paid $250 for every share. I set it to raise the $50 ,000. Before you go, I was going to ask you about Mr. Green.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he here from the way early days and established?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: He came from Kansas. I can't remember for sure how old he was. He homesteaded on the out here on the Barrage. He settled on Little Barrage. He had not to stand in a Yellard place, but the one right below it somewhere. Maybe it was the Leonard Nelson place, but it was out there on the ridge where he homesteaded.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever know what his connection was with this country that he came? Did he know anybody that was around here?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: No, there were several brothers that came from Canada.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had a connection in Canada, didn't they?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Well, he had a family in Canada. He had two brothers in Canada. I can't remember whether Dad was the first one or his mother and dad came from Virginia. They were out here as well and around Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's supposed to be related to what history?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: His mother was related to Lee. The General Lee. The second cousin. They were there, Virginia. We used to brag about it and go to school when we were in high school up here and brag about our famous relative. Dad said, you've got some that were hanged, too, so don't go bragging about it. You have some infamous ones, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he move into Troy when he started the roller mill?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Well, he farmed the knee out there. Yes, he farmed the knee on the farm. I don't know. He lived up here on the farm. This first house you go after you get up and top start down. That's the old family farm, too. The marriage has a third interest in that. He farmed there. Then he moved to town here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were born next door here?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Yes, I was born in this house next door. He married a gal that was what, 16 years old?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Yes, he eloped from out on Bear Ridge with my grandmother. I don't remember for sure if it was my step grandfather or my grandmother. He was hot in pursuit of them. But he got away. Dad was almost 20 years older than my mother. I made it. Yes, he lived happily. They had a good Moscow problem. They had to get to Justice and death. I wonder how he got into the roller mill. Well, that's quite a history in itself. He must have had the roller mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have a roller mill? Where did you ever hear that?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: That's what I thought this would have said in the first bank of Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever have a flour mill?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: It was a flour mill. That was that building right down there, then. Then he was older here, though. No, but he bought this from Duffy. They went to Spokane. I've heard him tell about that. There was a motor company there. That's when the transport motor truck was starting to be manufactured. He and Harry Driscoll and this August Johnson went to Spokane into that business. They bought, they formed a corporation, and they went into the business together all three of them. Green was a silent partner. He didn't go up. I don't know whether he was in the business or the Green business then or not. Anyway, they started this business, and then they broke up. August Johnson took the motor company himself. Harry Driscoll ended up with the Idaho Bean, and Frank Green continued on in the Ephraim Green company. That was a sideline. Don't any of those history books give much detail of his life?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: I don't think so. He was County Commissioner for years, and a lifelong Democrat. Thirty-second degree major.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a Democrat because he'd come from the South?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Probably, although he was in the Idaho State militia, because when they had the uprisings and the mines, I heard him tell about that they were called out. He had to go up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever tell about what happened to him up there?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: I think it was just the same as they called it a militia. Now they call it the National Guard. I think all they had to do was keep order. That was the size of it. That was when the IWWs and the miners were striking him then and so forth. But I don't think there was any lives lost or anything like that. He made his wealth just under the company. He made his wealth, obviously. He was wealthy before he must have gone into grain business. I think he was a conservative man that made money and was able to take care of it. That's how the old generation did it. Taking care of it was the main thing. But he stretched the old armor. He got a motion about farming. I would say so, yes, the early days. He was clearing land and selling it and branching out. I know the Cornwall place that he got, which was 160 acres. He financed the purchase of the— Have you interviewed Oscar Johansson? Does that name ring at all?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: His dad bought the place for $8 ,000, just $160. Frank Green gave him the $8 ,000 to buy it. He went down there. He lasted about four years. He couldn't even pay interest. So Andrew come to him, Well, I just as well deed it to you. He said, I can't make it. You can have it. Frank Green says, okay. He took it. Then he bought 40 acres from the Cornwall estate after that. So it's 200 acres out there. I know that's how he got that. But up here, I imagine he just naturally bought that from over a period of time. No, I got it. But during the grain business, it was fabulous. He was just like, I don't know, everybody. That office was always full of—stand there and talk. He was not a talker either. He was a quiet, reserved man, but his advice— But he was sure good with young people. Yeah, he could—and he was on the school board for years. But he would tell you, and it just seemed to be golden information. And a great advisor to his people, his customers, they all swore by him, you know. And of course, he would carry them. He'd give them their seed.

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: He just was more or less Santa Claus. He was mostly so when he bought the store. Well, that was the worst deal he ever got into. Put me in as manager after he couldn't stand the wait list, and they're not never had anything to do with the store in the line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which store is this?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: It's where the bus garages. It was just the one you mentioned to me once in the thirties, with the script on Rausch's— Yes, that's right. Our new man's this drawer. If you could call it that. You wouldn't call it that. No, that was a job. She managed it. She did a good job. But it was such a job because everything was credit. They all knew Frank Green, you know. And he had money. So I can't pay. You charge it. And that's the way it was. Yes, sir. Everybody charged. And he did well. He and Ollie Bowen had it together, actually. He and Ollie were 50-50.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they expect to make money on it?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: They were more interested because the store, it went broke. That was Williamson. The store went broke. And the bank had a bad note. The furniture, shoes. Grocery. You got a department store, boy. You could buy anything and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the same Williamson as it was in Moscow?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Same Williamson, yes. He came down here and he ran Williamson's department store. And he went broke during the thirties. And the bank had a note. And just for the end. And so Ollie, that's how Ollie got involved. And said, well, let's buy, let's take over the assets for the note. Because I think he paid all the creditors but the first bank could try. And so they, I don't think they ever did pay the note. But anyway, he and Frank Green started. They each dumped in, I think, each $2 ,000 in a pot. And that started the store. And they did a good business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of recovery was there on all those?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Well, Green and Bowman, I don't think they lost anything. They didn't make anything. But when it was all over, I don't know whether they fully recovered their original investment. I doubt it. I think they just forgot it. You say it was kind of a mess. I think so. Well, it was poor time. See, the depression wasn't over yet. Must have helped this town keep going. That was what they had in mind, was because they wanted a store. It was a great thing. That was when Saturday nights was fabulous.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Saturday nights?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Stay open till 12 o 'clock at night. And your store would be so full of people. Just like a fourth of July. Every Saturday. I think I wrote in that thing I gave you that you couldn't find a place to park on Saturday night. That was the truth. You couldn't. I worked in the store on Saturday nights.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, really?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Yes, I had helped Clark. We had a great time. We had the CC camp out here. I'll never forget to remember the kid that had his coat on. I noticed it was bulging. I happened to look in there and I could see color. I pulled it back and he had about two dozen ties. He was stealing them. I reprimanded him. I said, young man, I'm not going to do anything. You better not be caught in this store again. He pulled them all out and laid them on the counter. Out the door he went.

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Did you ever tell him about the gypsies?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: That would be Bertel Bolman. I wasn't in on that. I can tell you gypsy stories. Bertel Bolman. That's what they do to him. I don't know. They don't do the cash register for him. By the time they reach in, I don't think they ever got anything out. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You just don't trust them?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: They have to get in. This dynamite. I'll tell you one. When I first went to the bank, this cashier of this bank was shrewd. He was a Jew. He was good. But he couldn't conceive that any gypsy could ever get the best of him. Of course, they were so rare that he thought he was having a good time with the old bitty, the old squaw. Anyway, she had him to where he was putting $20 bill out on the counter. He had two of them out there. They were manipulating. Finally, he quit. He said, I've had enough. He put them back in the drawer. That night, we were $20 short. I'm so happy. He knew that that old gal got it. It's just as sure as a dick. I don't think he slept for a week. He didn't make any bones about Thomas about it. We had a cafe down at Kendrick George and I. We operated a cafe. They came in. They got me to open the till, too. But she was out. I wouldn't let her get to it. She'd take my hand. She'd say, hold my hand on my breast. Just like that. She had me going, too. The minute she stepped toward it, it was behind the counter on the confectionary bar. She'd take a step. I'd close it and I'd push her back. Finally, I got so scared that I said, you get out. She did. They had young girls running around the store. You don't know what they stole. They probably took every candy bar they could get their hands on. It has to do with telling your fortune. That happened to a friend of mine not too long ago in the city. Telling your fortune is how you lose a blessing of your money. That's right. A blessing of your money. That's how they get sight of it. I'll tell you one. This actually happened to George, our brother. I told you in that letter how we'd go home. If you ran into him, the mothers of parents, they always warned us, don't get near him because they steal boys and girls. We were told that and literally talked. Anyway, George and I was alone in a camp right below where we went up the hill to our farm. Just off the bridge of wage was a nice little camping spot at the head of the grade there. This was in the store, still daylight. George wanted to go down and talk to us. I didn't want to, but I wasn't going to let him go alone. I kind of followed behind. They came out. They were two older men. They started questioning George. They started telling us, we like boys like you. We could take the line on this and that. They were just kidding. They said to George, how far is it to the next town?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: He was a smart-alecky guy. He said, well, he says, George, it depends on how you travel. He'd been in a play, and this was part of the play. It depends on how you travel. They said, well, how's that?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: He said, well, if you walk, it's a long ways. If you ride, why?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: It's not too bad. You get there. What he says is, if you go on an airplane, you're practically there now. But if you'd have gone by telegraph, you'd been there already. When old Gypsy looked at him, he said, we don't want to hit that boy. You go on home. That actually happened. But he was a talking kid, you know. Dad always always had a time. George keeps his mouth shut. He was a politician in the family. That's just about for a Gypsy. If you go on a telegraph, you'd have been there already. But he'd learned to be in a play at school, and he pulled that on the Gypsy. I think usually they're the ones that get the best of you. They probably not get the best of you. Oh, they didn't steal kids, really. That was a way for parents to scare the kids that they don't get near them. Because they would be friendly. Gypsies were friendly. I don't care whether you were a kid. But oh, how they'd steal chickens. That's what they would come to your house in your yard, see. When the men would come to the doors, and the women would get into the chicken house, or get into the grain bins, they'd steal—they'd see their horses. They'd steal oats. They'd steal anything they'd get their hands on. Oh, it was awful. But you never knew when they were coming. The only way is if they come to one house, and then they call the next ones. Go out and shut the pasture gate.

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: And stand out there and just tell them that they don't want anything. Keep on going. That was common. But if you let them in near your house or in your barnyard, they would load their wagons up with grain or feed or anything to get there. They'd steal, steal, steal. And some of the women were really attractive as I recall. But they weren't dirty. They weren't well-dressed or anything like that. You were talking about—before something about town life in the 20s. And this store being open at night reminds me of that. I was thinking—we talked a little bit about the dances and a little bit about the bootlegging.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what did kids do?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Like when you were dating with Mrs. Brocke?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Well, of course. She taught school. And of course you might say she'd come home only every other week or whenever it was. But we went to dances. We went to dances a lot. We never missed—and after we got married and even before, we never missed a movie in Moscow. We always went to the movies. There wasn't a bad one ever. There never was a bad movie in those days. They were all good. We'd go to the movies. We'd go to the dances. We had a dance hall here. We had one in Helmer. So there was dancing. And there was plays. There was chautauquas. Or you'd put on a play yourself. That was common. I'd say that was—and of course, I had a car, so you would go or park or whatever you did. And you could go out into the rural. Every community had a schoolhouse, and they'd have some nights they'd have a dance, and they advertised that. You would go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it very different in the country than in town as far as the dances?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: No, no, no. I wouldn't say that. Your country dance would be strictly private. It'd be local people. In the dance hall at Helmer, the dance hall here at Troy was countywide. They'd come to players from Lewiston, and you'd see Pullman people. You'd see Genesee people come to the Allens Dance Hall in Troy and Moscow and so forth. The same way at Helmer. Helmer—I'd crack to them from Moscow and Pullman and Genesee all over. And that was a big affair. There'd be 200 people dancing at a time. There'd be crowds. There was a mammoth hall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you talking about the place at Helmer?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Yes. I was just talking to the Lawrances when they were out visiting us recently. Yes, I can remember that. They were telling me about running that place. They really had affairs out there. Yeah, it was a fad. It lasted so long, and when it blew up, it really blew. But of course, the Depression killed it because you catered to moonshine. You operated during the moonshine days. That was minor, but you never went to Helmer, but you had a good time. And as soon as 12 o 'clock came, that's when they had to stop, and they went to Rollerskating. You'd Rollerskate until 2 and 3 in the morning. I have gone—Marge and I have gone to Helmer with a party. It'd be about three and four couples of us. We'd never go to bed. That was after marriage. We'd take off as soon as the dance was over, and nobody in our particular party liked to Rollerskate. The fella said that was mostly for the teenagers or the half-drunks. But anyway, one family lived in Clarkia. That's Walt Johnson. He lived in Clarkia, and he was working for the forest there, so they had a house. So we got in the car, and we drove to Clarkia. Well, it'd be daylight. So you wouldn't even go to bed. You'd get breakfast, and then you'd go on out and start fishing, and you'd go fishing for the rest of the day. That's the life of it. It was fun. You thought nothing of it. That sounds great to me. That's right, yes.

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: But you made your own fun. There was plenty of opportunity. You could go. You could do. But it took somebody. It took friends. In other words, if you were a loner's, and you didn't run around with anybody, I would say it would be tough. Because you either went, you went to—when Helmer was good, there was no dance hall at Troy. It seemed as though that started dropping down, and the one was built here on the same pattern, the same sort of a deal as the one Helmer. Well, that lasted for about four or five years, and that was good. And so it would shut down for maybe two or three months, and somebody'd come along. Alan from Lewiston, who was a band leader, and he had his band. He'd come up, and he'd say, Alan, I'm going to try it again. And money was tight. Money was scarce. And it was still moonshine days. There was no liquor by the drink yet. That was still in the late 29s and 30s. See, the liquor didn't come until 34, 33 or 34, over the counter. So I was doing that law, and moonshine was tragic, because when you bought moonshine, you drank it. And you didn't throw it away, and you didn't take it home. You didn't dare have it—you had to drink it. And so consequently, they drank it, and they got drunker in hell because they had to get rid of it. That was the bad thing about it, see, because they were tough. There was law enforcement. There was squealers all around you. Guys, that was stool pigeons, they called them, you know. That really was a danger for you. Yes, it was, yes. You had poor—you just didn't go out and drink with a stranger. Because you didn't know about it. He was setting you up. He could blackboard you. Nothing else, right?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He could blackboard you?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Yes, he could say, well, we have a hall, we have him drink. And the third guy would come, see. His pal would come up and grab the bottle, and you would disappear, see. And you'd be standing there with a bottle. You'd be innocent. You thought you were drinking with a friend. That's how they pinned it on to you. And then in order to save your reputation or your name, oh, my gosh, you'd slip in $100 or whatever. He said, well, forget it. It's stunk. But those are the hazards of it. And then to drink more than you could take, I always got sick. I couldn't drink so much. I wouldn't let myself— and much whiskey and much moonshine as I had, I never got drunk. I always got sick before I did that. I heard that stuff was strong. No, no, no. It was much stronger than that. You couldn't drink it straight. You had to always cut it. Yeah, you never—if you got a bottle of moonshine, probably a third of it was moonshine. The rest would be ginger ale or— I don't know. I drank it with milk, even.

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: And put some—let's see. Milk… and cold milk wouldn't be bad. And I think—not ketchup,… but tomato juice was common. At first it was good because it helped your stomach. But I drank my share of moonshine. But you always wanted to get good. There was so much talk. Newspapers were constantly publishing a loss of sight and people dying for moonshine, bad moonshine. So you all were awful cautious of who you bought. I knew—I'd gone out here toward Randall Flat and you would send word out or you would tell him, I'll meet you here next Friday night. You always wanted your moonshine for Saturday. And they would deliver it at night. And you'd say, I'll meet you at such-and-such a corner. And my and my friend would go out there as far as we'd go with the car and he'd be—he would meet us. He'd be there waiting. And you'd drive up and then you'd pay him $2 .00 and a half, $3 .00 for a pint. And that pint went a long way. It was a good one time because you could cut it. You could make that pint into whatever you wanted, but you didn't dare keep it around. You had to dispose of it. You had to either give it to somebody else and say, well, I never sold any of my life. But you had to dispose of the evidence. So you drank it. And as a rule, you'd do your drinking before you, when you left your house in your home, see. And then you'd go to the dance. And then you drank enough that that lasted in there else. And there was always some wise guy out there that would have a bottle on him that was—or hid out there and you'd go outside. But as a rule, you had a good time. And you didn't have to be drunk to have it either. You didn't take a chance to drink your bottle. No, so you never saw a bottle inside. And you always buy it really out carefully. You bought away. Of course, there was bootleggers that would be the dancers. But you had to know who they are, and they had to know you. Or you'd have to take somebody with you that knew. But we never made that a practice. We'd get our drinking in our home, and we left. We'd leave Troy, say, at 9 o 'clock. We'd get to Elmer maybe by 10 or 9 .30. You danced till midnight, and you still felt good. It didn't take much to get to. And we were amateurs from the standpoint that we weren't heavy drinkers or hard drinkers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did any of the girls drink then?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Yes, but you'd never see one. Once in a while. But you'd take one drink or two drinks, they might take one. But they were might, and it didn't take much. But they didn't. As a rule, it was too strong for them, for them and most of them. And they couldn't stand it. It was hot. What was it that you mentioned to me about the guy that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was the guy that had been the doctor?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: He was the guy that was your family's doctor. He got sick just before he got the flu. And so he became incapacitated. And so in the midst of the epidemic, lo and behold here, this Dr. Kelly came into being. Who told him or whether...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What brought him to Kendrick?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: I don't know. But he had a horse and a buggy. And of course we had the telephone. And when our family became sick, he would cover a ridge at a time. In other words, he'd come up in the morning, and he'd see all the flu patients in the area. And then he would go back. And then the next morning, he'd go to the other ridge. And he was doing that. You probably could never get him unless there was an emergency. Only about every third day, because he had to cover a terrific territory. And he was alone. And he got by because he was a doctor. He professed to be a doctor. But anyway, he doctored us. And all he did was give the pills. And there was no science to it. There was not a heck of a lot you could do for flu. It's a dirty shame they didn't have whiskey, because I was told that that was one of the difficulties of the flu epidemic. Because they had whiskey. You know what I would have done?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: It stimulates into your heart. Your heart enlarges. And if you were a heavy, husky person, why they flew was much harder on you than the skinny one, because he had more room in there. But anyway, that's irrelevant. But anyway, after it was all over, people called him a horse doctor. They began to realize that he didn't do anything. And yet, when it was all over, you got the doctor bill and you paid him. And a lot of people just said that he was a horse doctor. He had no certificates, no license, and he had no office. So he hung around Kendrick, though, because he got to know quite a few people, and he got intimate. And Dr. Rothfeld went out of the business. That was the old family doctor. So Kendrick had none. So he kind of hung that out. And lo and behold, he had gone down right on Main Street in Kendrick. I think I could point the house to you right to this day. Anyway, he and his wife, I believe, obviously must have been a nurse or else some friend that knew the selling business. I don't say his wife was even involved, and she was a wonderful person. Anyway, he put a sign on the door, smallpox, keep out. And he operated that thing for over three months with that sign on there. Those people really got suspicious. And you could actually smell, you could smell the brine, you know, the cookies. And here he was manufacturing moonshine, and they caught him. And that was the end of Dr. Kelly.

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: But I can remember the sign. I remember seeing it, and I thought no nothing of it. But he left it there too, of all. But he was a real good player. But we got, there was several from Troy. That was caught in bootlegging. Herb Spencer out here. Have you ever interviewed him? No, I've talked to his brother, Jess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Should I talk to Herb too?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: He was arrested. He was put in jail. They caught him. He was moonshinin'. Yes, and a hard-working good man too. But he was one of the victims. Heck, you had to make him live in some way. So he did it. The McKeons were the big ones. That's what I remember. Oh, Sam McKeon. And he made good stuff. And they tried. He was, you know, he would just always want you up ahead of the goddamn reverent revenuers. And he manufactured it, and he sold it. He had several boys. They were all in cahoots. And Sam, he made good stuff. But he was touchy. If he didn't know you, he would never sell to me because I was Frank Green's son-in-law. That was enough. I don't give a goddamn. I won't sell you nothing. I know Frank Green. I don't think his son-in-law should have it. That's just to be his attitude, see. But Herman Boyd, he was the old reliable. That's where I bought all mine from, first me. He made wonderful stuff. You knew it was good. Did I ever tell you that I was a kid who graduated from school up here. He graduated in May from high school. And I played against him in basketball. It was my first year in Troy.

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: He must have graduated—no, he had already been out. Wilford Johnson. A great big guy. But anyway, I kept the books. Besides the bank books, I kept the books for Sam Olsen and Edwin Johansson. They had a garage across the street from the bank. So after banking hours, I would go over there and do the posting, post their ledgers, and send out the statements and so forth on their books. So I went over there, probably 4 .30 in one afternoon. And here I went and went into their garage. They had a service station, and they had a garage behind. And I walked in the door, and here was—oh, I'd say one, two, three, four. There was a good eight men standing around, setting on anything they could get up. Some of them were standing. And in the center on an apple box was a five-gallon can. And that was full of moonshine. And it belonged to this Wilford Johnson. And they had each one—they had given each one a sample, and he wanted to sell it. And that was so bad that there was only one guy that could even begin to drink it. And that was an alcoholic by the name of Eskel Gord. That's George Gord's brother. He would up that guy named Cupp, and he would rush it around his mouth, just like you would water, and swallow. No, he wasn't good, but it wasn't bad. He bought that whole five-gallon for five bucks. And I saw that. And the rest of them, they wouldn't know more than buying up than nothing, because it was poison to them. And of course that man died eventually from his drinking. But he was a real moonshine-drinking man. Eskel Gord never made any. He always bought it. And he was well-to-do, too. He was worth money. Sam McKeon really has a fine— Sam McKeon. Oh, he was a moonshiner, was the word go. And they caught him. They would keep picking him up on general principles, on us. They knew he was making it. They knew he was selling it. They knew he was peddling it. And they'd pick him up on the smallest charges. But he was clever. He was good at it. I drank his stuff. It was good. But I never bought it. As I say, he wanted to sort me nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it usually the county sheriffs like Summerfield and Pat Moody?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Pat Moody was the watchdog. See, Pat Moody worked under Summerfield. He was his deputy. And Pat Moody was—and so was Jordan. Moody was smart, and Jordan was nothing but a bull. I saw Jordan one day right out here. It was a dirt road over this gravel. It wasn't boiled. It was right out here. And he stopped a young kid. And he suspicious that he had whiskey in his car and that he was drunk. And it was a touring car open, you know, open side. And I saw him pull the door open and grab that kid by the nap of the neck, by his shirt and the nap of the neck, and just lift him right out of the car and set him right out on the gravel. Mad on. I honest to goodness. I lost respect for him for law and order. That kid, he never had nothing on him, nothing at all, but just because he may have had a maybe a little bit shady reputation. But he was going to just beat the hell out of that kid. He had asked him some questions, and he hadn't got the proper answer. He didn't think, and that's what he was going to do. That kid was no more drunk than nothing. And before he got through it, the kid was back in the car, and he drove off. And Jordan was still hollering or snorting like he did, you know. He had no good for anybody. He didn't have a good word for his own mother, I don't think. I never liked Jordan. Just incidents like that just made you really sick of him. I've heard he was kind of the heavy. Yes, he was the heavy. Half moody was not that tight. Half moody was a... He had a heart. He had conscience. He was clever, though. He was smart. He would just as good as any dog in the country, and cracking down a still. And he ate a lot of guts. I know I've talked to him. He's told me about... He liked to... I guess a lot of times he would impersonate a lumberjack or something like that. Yeah, he'd join the disguise, yeah. He no doubt was a very good undercover man. And so little, you wouldn't even think he was a... That's right, yeah. No, no, he was a wiry dog.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I suppose I should get going What time do you have there?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: A quarter till five. Probably I should, because you have a pretty big... Baseball was great in my day when I was still an eighth grader, you might say, or seventh grader. Because on the ridges, there was baseball in the backyard, in the cow lots, and in the Timothy fields. That was common. I had two cousins, and there was two of us, George and I, and Tony, and Otto Eichner. And then we'd have Harry Amling, and you'd have Tim Wade. But even the four of us, we could have a ballgame. Those two are our two cousins, and whenever we'd go to our aunts and uncles for dinner, we played baseball. And of course, the Eichner's father was quite an enthusiast. And so Moscow had a baseball team. They called it the Moscow Cubs. It was made up, and it was managed by a man by the name of Eastman. And Kony Estes was one. Frazier, this Frazier in Spokane, he was a player. I played against him. But anyway, we organized. We got nine ballplayers at Kenry. And we, off of American Ridge, and these Eichner boys were good. One of them could pitch. Otto was a pitcher, and he could throw a curve ball at you. And I was playing against that constantly, so I knew how to bat against the, I'm not bragging, because we all did. He threw a curve, and it'd come right at you, and then it would curve over the plate. And you'd be out here dodging, see?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: And he'd throw that in against these Cubs, and he had them scared to death. And they were just backing away from the plate, and they'd curve over, and it'd be strike. And in one game, they never got onto him. And Moscow had a pitcher, and he did the same thing. But we'd stand up there and watch the ball and curve. And I got up to bat four times, and every one of them, I was striking fast, see?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: And every one of them went over third base, and I got a hit. And we all did. We beat them. I think the spar was 7-3, something like that. And we really got the reputation, because Moscow Cubs was really prominent. They had baseball suits. We went up there in our clothes, in our short pass, or whatever it had. And on the way up, never forget this, we stopped the train, and it came to Kendrick at 9 o 'clock. We came through Troy, and we got to Moscow about 10 or between 10 and 11. We played in the afternoon, and then we got on the train, and we was back into Kendrick at 9 o 'clock that night. That was the schedule, see?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: We had two trains a day both ways. Anyway, we got to Troy, and we had only eight players. And so we stuck our head out the window, and there was a kid on the platform over there. We hollered at him, hey, can you play baseball?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: He said, yeah, a little. He was skinny, and his name was Weeks. And I swear to you, he had TB. He was skinny. Well, come on, you only go to Moscow and play, we'll buy your ticket. And we got that kid, and he went up there and played for us. That was the ninth player, believe it or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he any good?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Oh, I can't. Sam, he just killed a spot. But the idea was, knowing him, after I came to Troy, I remembered him. I remembered him that well, and I kidded him about it. And so then you put two and two together, one and a half on the same hill. We hollered at you, didn't your parents miss you?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: And you get up here and you find out his background. He went home whenever he wanted to go home. He was kind of a rounder and a troubled kid. And so he fell right in mind. But he swear, I think, I still think that's Murrow Weeks out here, at the same kid. He works for the university. I don't know, he works for Washburn, Wilson Seed, and all. But I think he's the same one. It either has to be that or else it was Vernon. It was one of those kids. And they were just little devils at that time. But we went up there and we won that game.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how regular did you have a regular team?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Well, we played every Sunday among ourselves. But the following year, we only paid the most cubs once then, because it was so hard to get there. But they come down. After that, they were so impressed with this Eichner pitching. And his brother was good at everything too. And they hired. They hired. I say they came down every Sunday and they got this because they wanted to give this boy an opportunity. He was that good at pitching because he had a fabulous curve. And they took him and he joined the cub team, he and his brother. And of course, our dad died that winter, so we were out. And so there was no more baseball for us then. But they did. And then Otto died the following year. He got pneumonia.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Otto Weichner?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Mm-hmm. He died. It was either pneumonia or else, as I recall, I think it was a burst of dependence. And they put him on the train in Kendrick and they took him to Moscow and he died doing the operation. And that was the last of Otto. But Tony became quite an outstanding ballplayer. He played for Ritzville, he played for Moscow, he played for Genesee. He was in demand too. He was a good little ballplayer. He could have had, he had ever followed it. I think he could have made big lead. But he was so small. He was a small guy. But of course, size was no big issue in those days if you were good. But he didn't pitch and he didn't catch, but he played outfield. I think he played shortstop because he's regular. But he was pretty good at the bat. I was going to ask you if Kendrick and Troy, when you were growing up, if they had days like, not baseball, I'm thinking of celebration days. Oh, yes. Yeah, they had a Fourth of July. Not very often. It got to be such a chore, you know. The celebrations were fantastic. When you did put on a Fourth of July, oh, it was really spontaneous, large. They'd have speakers, you know. Politicians would come there and the entertainment would be thrown at the nigger babies. And I can recall, I think I wrote that in that thing that I wrote up. We went to Moscow to a Fourth of July. It was either Fourth of July or a county fair. Probably was a, I don't know which one. Whether it was a Fourth of July or the county fair anyway, my dad and all of us kids, Kenneth, I don't think he was even born then. But there was four of us kids, three boys and one girl, and mother and dad. And we got on the train in Kendrick at nine o 'clock in the morning and we went to Moscow to the fair. And my biggest impression was when I had my dad stood beside me and we watched the people throw at a nigger behind a curtain. He was behind a canvas and his head stuck through the hole. His hands were behind. Just his head was out. And they threw baseballs at him. I would say that they threw as far as from here to the kitchen. That would be that much. He had plenty. He never got hit. But ever so often you could get eggs instead of the balls if you wanted to pay more money. See, that was supposed to be more fun to throw a rotten egg at him. That's what they called it. For three for a quarter, rotten eggs. Otherwise the baseballs was maybe three for a dime because there was no loss there. But the eggs were gone. And then they throw eggs at him every so often. See, you get somebody, but give him a quarter while he could have eggs to throw. And I felt so sorry for that boy. And I made me sick, see. And just to watch it, the expression on my face must have been something as I think about it now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that expression on his face?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Oh, he was having the time of his life. Sure. No, he was being paid for it. But he was hollering. He'd holler at him and they'd call him your dirty black son of a bitch. Doesn't that?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: No women around. Women would be away from it. But they, all the men were having the time of their lives. And he was too. He dodged. They never hit him. Only with the eggs. The eggs would break, right?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Say maybe that far from him. They'd splash in his face. He didn't care. He was, I don't know what he got, but it was just the idea to me. And I never forgot it. Isn't that funny?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, it sounds so. And in those days, there was no, there was no, nothing taught in our school. We never did see a Negro. There was as rare as chickens, you know. We never saw, and we saw a lot of Indians because we were in an Indian area. We had a Chinese man in Kendrick who lived there. Gene Chinaman. Well, I'd never met him before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right? Yeah, what was, what he'd do?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: He was a vegetable gardener. He had his garden. My dad, we never went by his house but what we would stop. And he'd come out or he'd be crossing the street and dad had talked to him. And he wanted, he liked us because we were boys. Chinese, they had no use for girls. And he would tell dad that. He'd be like, you know, girl, I take those boys anytime. You want to leave them here, you go ahead. And of course, dad would get a kick out of that, see. And we'd be a little bit scared, but we weren't, you know, as long as we were with him or when we were safe. But he would talk. He was just a kind old, clean gentleman. And he lived for, you know, after we moved to Kendrick, I was in his, I've been in his house, in his shack, as you might say, no more than a small, I'd say it wasn't as large as that garage. His home, full, but warm and comfortable, clean. There was a kitchen and a bed. Everything was in this room. He had a wood floor. He lived all by himself. Lived all by himself. No, the Chinese people there. Never, I don't think he ever even had a visitor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have a big garden?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Yes, fabulous. And he'd wheel it uptown. He'd put it in a two-wheel cart. And he was constantly, as the stuff would come right, he'd deliver it to the stores. You never bought your wreck from him, unless, if you wanted to, fine. But he patronized the stores because he sold to the stores and the stores would sell to customers. And so he would never, I don't know whether, we never did, and I don't know whether anybody ever would stop and buy direct from him because I think the stores took everything he had and they liked it that way, see, then they got to sell it and make the profit on it. But he would raise cabbages, corn, tomatoes, not too much cantaloupe or watermelons. I don't think he did that at all. I can just see his garden right across the street from where he went. I wonder if he rented it. No, he was on it. Well, the garden, I don't know what deal he had there because it was... It's pretty tougher than to buy land, which I guess. Yes, and the fact that nobody wanted this because water would set in it pretty late in the spring so that you didn't build a house there. There was no dwelling that's close to it. And I think that was the reason that's how he happened to get it. And he knew how to farm wet land. And his house was on the railroad right away so nobody could touch him. If you go down to Kendrick, where the Brocke warehouses, that's on railroad right away. And you get down there to where that road where you hit Kendrick and that road comes in, all those houses on there are on the NP railroad right away. They pay so much a year. It's a personal property. All they can sell, if they ever sell, is their house. They can't sell alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he stayed there for quite a while?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Well, he was there. He was there. Gene Chinaman, I'd say, was obviously there 20 to 25 years because he was old when I was... And my dad still lived. And he died after I came to Troy, I'm sure, which had been early in the late 20s. I don't think he was even alive in the Depression. Kenny would probably know my brother. How long he actually lived, I don't know. But he was an old man in 1918. And that's when my dad was alive. And we saw him because he was slow. And he was... Oh, he was just an old man, period. But he was a Chinaman. A lot of those people always planned on going back to China. He never did. Yeah, but he... I don't know how he got there. I don't know. You never... Nobody questioned him. Nobody visited him. I think kids were afraid that Gene Chinaman chopped your ears off. And he didn't want kids to hang around him, you know, because they never molested him. Never broke his windows. They never stole from him. And at night, you wouldn't even think of going there, there. And at daytime, why... He had nothing. You weren't at ease after I got old enough when I was in high school. And as I say, I'd go down there and walk in and talk to him. He was glad to have me. But he knew me too, because he remembered me. He remembered my dad. He remembered you from growing up. Yes, so I was... But I never did anything for him. And he never asked me to do it. I was so young, you know, I was still in high school. But you get the idea. He really didn't have much to do with people. No, he didn't. And that was for sure. Yes. In fact, I don't even know what happened to him after... After he became ill and died, I don't know where he's buried. I have no idea. I never even give him a thought, you know. He was just a forgotten man. A lot of them really wanted to go back to be buried in China. I would imagine. And he obviously had to have money, because he was so frugal. And other than his salt and his pepper and his flour and how bet you he didn't buy anything.

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: And his cost of his garden, which was always fabulous. Oh, he had a fantastic garden. Unbelievable. He really could raise. He knew how. He didn't give it away. He always sold it. And the merchants would cater to him, because they wanted him. It was good. They sent, from what I've learned from talking to this older Chinese couple, they sent the money back to China a bunch of the time. Because the families were really poor. And that's why they were here, because they could make some money here and send them back. And if you had somebody in your family here and they were making money, you were pretty awesome. You're expected, yeah. I think that's true in the Jews, too. I think they do that, yet. I don't think there's any question that we're keeping Israel just on general principle. You know, and I never knew to this day, I don't know what Gene Chinaman's name was. That's all I ever heard, is Gene Chinaman. Obviously, his name was Gene, and the Chinaman was picked on because that's what everybody called him. Nobody ever said Gene, nobody ever said Chinaman. That's Gene Chinaman. Isn't that funny?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: But now that we talked about it, Marvin Mullen, I knew so much about him and heard him tell, Herman Paulson, tell about other people. And for some reason, I don't remember it,… which makes history to me very interesting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All right, well, that's the only link that there is, isn't that the people who remember?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: No, but every year they're dropping off. And for some reason or other, there's a gap. There's a gap between real old and those out of us that don't think we're old, see?

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: And just like this, Jennie Driscoll, I think you're going to, I don't want you to do it because I think you're going to be amazed. And if you just, and I told her, he's not going to write. You just talk, you just forget that he's there. And you just ramble on. You can tell him anything you want. But those incidents, like you tell about riding that horse, those are things that are going to take the attraction. That's going to make people laugh and think, well, that maybe happened to them. And I said, and I said, you go home now and you think about it. And now I get Sam there just as quick as we can. And she says, okay, but she says, I'm afraid it might be disappointing or it won't. And she walked three, it took her three hours to go to school, to walk to school. And that's something else that should be brought out. Yeah, I don't, I mean, 20 years ago would have been fantastic. Oh, man, there was so much history then.

speaker: Frank Brocke or Margie Brocke
words: Yes, sir… so, so I think you've got the ball rolling. For goodness sake, don't let it stop. Yeah, I'm figuring on.
